text: Hi, I am Dr. Sandeep Naik. I am a laparoscopic and robotic cancer surgeon. In this video, I am demonstrating a case of pancreatic duodenectomy in a proven case of peri-ampullary carcinoma. This procedure has been performed totally robotically.

This surgery involves removal of head of pancreas, duodenum, portion of stomach and gallbladder, following which the continuity of these organs is restored by the formation of pancreatic jejunostomy, choledochore jejunostomy and gastro jejunostomy.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac by incising the gastrocolic omentum, followed by mobilizing the colon up to the hepatic flexure to fully expose the duodenal C-loop.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the right gastroepiploic artery and the regional lymph nodes below the pylorus, moving towards the pancreas. The pylorus and the first part of the duodenum are exposed, with dissection occurring posteriorly.

The surgeon identifies the communicating branch between the middle colic vein and the right gastroepiploic vein, taking care to avoid accidental tearing that could cause heavy bleeding. The inferior pancreatic urinal vein is exposed and carefully ligated.

The surgeon uses the butt of the robotic scissors to tunnel around the superior mesenteric vein, carefully lifting the pancreas with robotic forceps. The tunneling is performed on the anterior aspect, where typically no branches drain into the vein, minimizing the risk of injury.

The surgeon performs complete caucarization of the duodenum, approaching from below upwards. The dissection follows along the ligament of Treitz, and the jejunum is delivered into the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon transects the jejunum at a sufficient length, followed by the clearing of lymph nodal structures in the porta hepatis. The left hepatic artery is exposed and being cleared of surrounding tissue.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and cuts the right gastric artery, exposing the gastroduodenal artery, which is also clipped and cut. The portal vein is visualized between the common hepatic duct and the common hepatic artery. The common hepatic duct is dissected, secured with an endo-bulldog clamp, and cut at a sufficient length to prevent bile spillage.

The surgeon completes the tunneling of the superior mesenteric vein and passes a tape through the tunnel. The first part of the duodenum is transected using a stapler, with care taken to preserve the pylorus. The pancreas is also transected.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder as one of the final steps of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process, a challenging and vascular area, using the third robotic arm to retract the superior mesenteric vein. The branches and tributaries leading to the superior mesenteric vessels are ligated. Below the vein, the superior mesenteric artery is exposed, requiring careful handling to avoid injury. The superior pancreaticoduodenal vein, draining into the portal vein, is secured with clips and cut, completing the dissection of the uncinate process.

The surgeon cannulates the small pancreatic duct using a six French infant feeding tube. A pancreaticojejunostomy is performed with 4-0 BDS sutures, starting with a continuous suture for the posterior layer.

The surgeon performs a jejunostomy, placing interrupted pedius sutures between the pancreatic duct and the jejunostomy site. An infant feeding tube is used as a stent and left in situ, completing the ductomucosa anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the fourth layer of anastomosis using PDS 4-0 interrupted sutures. The bulldog clamp is released from the bile duct, and an anastomosis is formed between the jejunum and the bile duct. Finally, a gastro-jejunostomy is performed.
